questions.question: Explain the composition of e-waste into four major material categories and give one example of each.
keyPoints: Ferrous metals (iron/steel) - from computer casings, chassis
Non-ferrous metals (copper, aluminum) - from wiring, heat sinks
Plastics - from casings, cables, connectors
Glass - from CRT screens, LCD panels with lead/mercury

questions.question: List five hazardous substances found in e-waste and their health effects on humans.
keyPoints: Lead - neurological damage, cognitive impairment
Mercury - brain damage, kidney failure
Cadmium - bone damage, cancer risk
Brominated flame retardants - endocrine disruption
PVC燃烧产生的二恶英 - 致癌、免疫毒性

questions.question: What is the concept of 'Urban Mining'? How does it relate to e-waste management?
keyPoints: Urban mining = recovering valuable materials from waste
E-waste contains 40-50x more gold than gold ore
Also recovers copper, silver, palladium, rare earths
Reduces need for virgin mining, conserves resources

questions.question: Differentiate between CRT and LCD displays in terms of composition and hazardous content.
keyPoints: CRT: Contains 2-4 kg lead in glass, barium, phosphorus
LCD: Contains mercury in backlights, indium in touchscreens
CRT heavier, more lead; LCD lighter but mercury hazard
Both require specialized recycling processes

questions.question: Calculate the approximate amount of copper in 1000 kg of e-waste if non-ferrous metals constitute 10% of e-waste and copper is 60% of non-ferrous content.
keyPoints: Non-ferrous metals = 1000 × 10% = 100 kg
Copper = 100 × 60% = 60 kg
Answer: 60 kg of copper
Shows significant recoverable resource value

questions.question: What are the three main sources of e-waste generation? Explain each with examples.
keyPoints: Households: Personal computers, mobile phones, TVs, appliances
Business/Industry: Office IT equipment, servers, manufacturing scraps
Government/Public: School computers, healthcare equipment, telecommunications

questions.question: Explain why only about 17-22% of global e-waste is formally recycled despite 70-80% being technically recyclable.
keyPoints: Lack of collection infrastructure in many regions
Informal sector dominates in developing countries
Hazardous content discourages handling
Economic viability issues for low-value plastics
Lack of awareness among consumers

questions.question: Discuss the global statistics on e-waste generation, regional variations, and the factors contributing to the rapid growth of e-waste. Suggest solutions to manage this crisis.
keyPoints: Global generation: 62 million tonnes annually, growing 3-4% per year
Asia generates highest volume (45%), Africa lowest but growing
Factors: Short product lifecycles (2-3 years for phones), technological obsolescence, affordability, consumerism
Solutions: EPR policies, proper collection infrastructure, formal recycling facilities, awareness, design for recycling

questions.question: Describe the composition of a typical desktop computer by percentage weight. Explain why proper e-waste management is critical considering both resource recovery and environmental protection.
keyPoints: Composition: Ferrous metals 20-25%, Plastics 20-30%, Copper 3-5%, Aluminum 2-4%, Circuit boards 6-8%, Glass 10-15%
Resource recovery: Precious metals (Au, Ag, Pd), rare earths in magnets
Environmental protection: Prevents leaching of lead, mercury, cadmium into groundwater
Health protection: Prevents toxic exposure to workers in informal recycling
Economic potential: Urban mining can recover billions in resources

questions.question: Explain the WEEE directive categorization of e-waste into 10 categories. Why is categorization important for effective e-waste management?
keyPoints: 10 Categories: 1) Large appliances 2) Small appliances 3) IT/telecom 4) Consumer equipment 5) Lighting 6) Electrical tools 7) Toys/sports 8) Medical devices 9) Monitoring instruments 10) Automatic dispensers
Importance: Different categories have different material compositions
Different hazardous materials require different handling
Recycling technologies vary by category
Facilitates proper collection, treatment, and resource recovery
